title: Fable 5 Shut Down by US Government
dek: A national-security directive forced Anthropic to abruptly cut off Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all foreign nationals worldwide — the first time the US has hit the kill switch on a frontier model. The pretext is a thin jailbreak; the real story is a precedent that splits the world by model access and turns Anthropic's safety rhetoric into the rope it hangs by.

oneIdea.headline: The Fable shutdown wasn't about a jailbreak — it was a kill switch, and Anthropic spent a year loading it.
oneIdea.summary: The US government invoked national-security export controls to force Anthropic to disable Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for every foreign national on earth — including its own non-citizen employees — over a narrow jailbreak that surfaced already-public vulnerabilities. The industry's fury splits two ways at once: the government's pretext is cartoonishly thin and its export strategy incoherent, and Anthropic spent a year insisting its models were too dangerous for anyone but itself to be trusted with — then acted shocked when Washington took that premise literally. Either way, the precedent is the story: model access is now a sovereign weapon, and no lab's deployment is safe from a 5 PM Friday directive.

gpCorner.note: AI commentary for UK general practice; not from the podcast

items.title: A 5 PM Friday could take your scribe offline
items.body: The story here is a frontier model switched off worldwide overnight by a single government letter. Your AI scribe almost certainly sits on top of a US lab's model. If NLW is right that the economy now leans on these labs, that concentration is itself the systemic risk; if Zitron is right that they are unprofitable and racing to float, the more likely failure is the vendor folding or repricing once subsidies end. Either reading points to the same precaution: don't let a single foreign model become load-bearing in clinical workflow without a manual fallback and a clear answer to 'what do we do the morning it stops working.'

items.title: Where does the patient data sit, and for how long
items.body: Anthropic argued it needs thirty days of retained customer data to catch jailbreaks. Set aside the politics: any tool that holds identifiable consultation content for a retention window is an information-governance question for the practice, not just the supplier. Ask your scribe vendor plainly where UK patient data is processed and stored, for how long, and whether a foreign government directive could reach it. The Fable episode shows those are no longer theoretical concerns.

items.title: The case for sovereign or UK-hosted tooling just got stronger
items.body: The sharpest line in the brief was that model access is becoming a question of citizenship. For the NHS that is an argument — not yet urgent, but real — for preferring clinical AI that can run on UK or European infrastructure, or that is portable between models. It is the same procurement discipline as last week: shorter terms, exit rights, and the ability to move your templates and data if the ground shifts under a single provider.

the_other_side.text: The loudest economic claim in today's brief is NLW's — that the whole US economy now rests on Anthropic and OpenAI revenue rising and investors continuing to fund the buildout, which is why the Fable shutdown is so dangerous. Writing the day before, Zitron argues the dependency is the danger: both labs have just filed to go public precisely because they 'burn billions of dollars a year and have no path to profitability,' racing for what he calls 'exit liquidity' before the bill lands. The trillion-dollar valuations NLW says are 'now in question' were, on his telling, never sound to begin with. Where NLW sees a national asset a clumsy government must stop endangering, Zitron sees the bubble itself — and a government merely poking it a little early.

the_truth_beneath.text: Strip the rhetoric and both men orbit one variable: whether the frontier labs have a path to profit or merely a path to more capital. They accept the same facts — Anthropic and OpenAI have both filed to go public, both burn billions a year, and trillion-dollar valuations now hang on events neither company controls. The empirical question that will resolve is whether the flotation filings show margins bending toward profit or losses widening; the audited prospectus financials are the observable that settles it. For NLW to be vindicated, revenue must scale and the buildout stay funded, so 'the economy rests on this' holds. For Zitron, the filings must expose the dog — no margin, endless burn, exit liquidity dressed as growth. Both are polemicists: NLW sells optimism and the anti-doom case, Zitron sells bubble-certainty and anti-hype. The signal is where they agree despite opposite priors — both now call the valuation precarious. Watch the prospectus.

headlines.headline: US government orders Fable 5 and Mythos 5 shut down
headlines.summary: Citing national security authorities, the government issued an export control directive suspending all access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 by any foreign national, inside or outside the US — including Anthropic's own foreign-national employees. The net effect: Anthropic had to abruptly disable both models for all customers. Other Claude models are unaffected.

headlines.headline: We believe this is a misunderstanding and are working to restore access as soon as possible.
headlines.attribution: Anthropic, in its statement on the suspension
headlines.summary: Anthropic's public statement framed the shutdown as an error rather than a justified safety action, apologizing to customers while pledging to get the models back online.

headlines.headline: Lutnick's letter put the models under export restrictions
headlines.summary: The Wall Street Journal reported Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick sent CEO Dario Amodei a letter declaring Fable 5 and Mythos 5 subject to export restrictions — barring use by customers outside the US and by foreign nationals within it.

headlines.headline: The pretext: a narrow jailbreak that just reads code and fixes flaws
headlines.summary: Anthropic says the directive arrived at 5:21 PM with no specific details, but rests on a method of bypassing Fable 5. On review, the technique surfaced only a few previously-known minor vulnerabilities that other publicly available models find too — essentially asking the model to read a codebase and fix software flaws.

headlines.headline: This standard would essentially halt all new model deployments for all frontier model providers.
headlines.attribution: Anthropic, in its blog post on the directive
headlines.summary: Anthropic argued perfect jailbreak resistance is impossible for any provider, so recalling a model deployed to hundreds of millions over a narrow potential jailbreak sets an unworkable bar for the entire industry.

headlines.headline: The jailbreak research traces back to Amazon
headlines.summary: The WSJ reported the research was done by Amazon researchers — an Anthropic investor and Project Glasswing partner — who used a prompt series to extract info on a handful of known security vulnerabilities. Notably, the Journal did not report that Amazon shared the findings with the government.

headlines.headline: Anthropic reportedly told the government to pound sand
headlines.summary: Per Prinz's timeline assembled from Axios reporting, the government contacted Anthropic to ask it to pause releasing the models and was unsuccessful — a refusal many read as setting up the confrontation that followed.

headlines.headline: Even Anthropic's own staff can't touch the models
headlines.summary: A large share of Anthropic's technical staff — including names like Andrej Karpathy — are not US citizens but here on visas such as EB-1. Under the directive, those employees are barred from interacting with Fable 5 and Mythos 5 internally.

headlines.headline: Am I mad at Anthropic or the US government? Both? Probably both.
headlines.summary: Dan Robustus on X captured the whole tenor of the discourse — a fight in which almost no party emerges looking good.

headlines.headline: The US banned Fable just because it responded with information already freely available on the internet.
headlines.summary: AI entrepreneur Bindu Reddy called the move 'really stupid,' noting any model can be coaxed into discussing common security vulnerabilities — and the cluelessness of the government, she said, is astounding.

headlines.headline: I can't tell if this is lawfare against Anthropic or extreme national security hawkery. Regardless, it's simply cartoonish.
headlines.summary: AI policy expert Dean Ball voiced a widely shared confusion about the directive's actual motive — and a shared verdict on its competence.

headlines.headline: Across-the-board controls on all countries on a single model without any warning is highly questionable.
headlines.attribution: Chris Maguire, Council on Foreign Relations
headlines.summary: CFR's Chris Maguire backs targeted model export controls but blasted Commerce and BIS for an 'incoherent and self-defeating' strategy — sending chips to China while blocking US companies from releasing their own models.

headlines.headline: The White House's own words come back to haunt it
headlines.summary: Just weeks earlier, the White House OSTP account had pushed back on reporting about AI oversight, insisting it was not conducting oversight of all new models — because that 'level of government overreach would have chilling effects on free speech and innovation.' Critics resurfaced it as direct hypocrisy.

headlines.headline: Some things are simply more important than revenue cycles, clickbait, and pre-IPO valuation.
headlines.attribution: Kirsten Davies, Department of War CIO
headlines.summary: Department of War CIO Kirsten Davies posted in support of the action — a pointed shot at Anthropic that, to many, signaled the dispute is about the government's relationship with the company, not Fable 5 itself.

headlines.headline: The arrogance with which Anthropic has pursued the latest release has universally landed poorly.
headlines.summary: AI builder Sarah Hooker captured the industry's scorn: the posture that everyone should be grateful to touch an intentionally hobbled technology — and that no one else should be allowed to build it because it's too dangerous.

headlines.headline: Dario got the regulation he asked for
headlines.summary: Critics resurfaced an Anthropic blog line arguing the government 'should have the power to block or deter deployment' of risky models. Will Manidis summarized the whiplash: Dario 48 hours ago said the government should be able to block deployments — and now that it has, says 'not like that.' Anthropic's caveat: it wanted a transparent, fair statutory process, which this wasn't.

headlines.headline: I can't believe Anthropic comparing their product to nuclear weapons 800 times backfired on them. I am shocked.
headlines.summary: Investor Nick Carter's sarcasm summed up a dominant view: Anthropic's relentless danger framing stoked the very regulatory panic that just took its flagship models offline.

headlines.headline: They named it Fable and then acted surprised when it came with a moral.
headlines.summary: Banteg's one-liner became the episode's most concise indictment of Anthropic's self-inflicted predicament.

headlines.headline: The government is starting to deem some models too powerful for certain uses, which creates a precedent for a range of possible controls.
headlines.summary: Aaron Levie called it a major turning point for AI regulation — and argued we're unlikely to return to a world where the government isn't far more involved in the pace of AI progress.

headlines.headline: Citizenship checks could ripple all the way downstream
headlines.summary: Brian Zhao argued restoring access may require ID/citizenship verification not just on Claude but everywhere Fable is served — Cursor, Devin, OpenRouter, a law firm on Harvey. He also noted OpenAI and Google now have little incentive to ship anything Mythos-caliber, since any jailbreak could trigger the same export controls.

headlines.headline: This is Anthropic's 'Altman firing' moment with the government
headlines.summary: NLW compared it to when Sam Altman was ousted and reinstated at OpenAI — the moment Microsoft began quietly building resilience outside OpenAI's models. Once a partner proves itself too capricious to trust, the relationship is permanently reshaped, even if it nominally continues.

headlines.headline: We fought this battle in the '90s for free and open access to cryptography... the fight this time will be much harder.
headlines.summary: Connor Brown framed the moment as the opening of the AI wars — KYC and 'anti-compute laundering' laws for frontier models — and asked whether the public will ever have access to frontier intelligence again.

headlines.headline: The whole US economy now rests on this relationship staying intact
headlines.summary: NLW argued the American economy effectively depends on Anthropic and OpenAI revenue rising and investors continuing to fund the buildout. The damage from this move, he said, isn't just to Anthropic — it's hard to overstate the hit to the entire economy.

headlines.headline: The 'sovereign AI is real' moment arrives
headlines.summary: Commentators argued the shutdown is a warning shot for middle powers: frontier access is not guaranteed. Gale Weiner noted the US narrative advantage over China — predictable, rule-of-law provider versus arbitrary actor — just evaporated, handing procurement officers worldwide a defensible case for sovereign AI and even Chinese open-weight alternatives.

headlines.headline: This is a new kind of Iron Curtain — digital, intellectual.
headlines.summary: Mal on X warned the government is creating a caste system based on access to intelligence: a divide not between rich and poor but between those allowed to think at the frontier and those who simply happen to be citizens of another country.
